professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: We've been discussing how assessment shapes learning this semester. A few schools have replaced letter grades with written narrative feedback—detailed comments on what a student has mastered and what still needs work. Some educators say this deepens learning and eases anxiety, while others worry it leaves students without a clear benchmark. Should schools drop letter grades in favor of narrative feedback? Why or why not?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I believe narrative feedback genuinely improves learning. A letter grade reveals almost nothing about which specific skills a student lacks, whereas detailed comments point toward concrete next steps. When students stop fixating on a single symbol, they tend to engage with the material far more thoughtfully.

students.name: Paul
students.text: In my opinion, abandoning grades creates more confusion than clarity. Narrative comments are subjective and time-consuming, and busy teachers may end up writing vague, formulaic remarks. A grade, whatever its flaws, gives students and universities a consistent benchmark for comparison that paragraphs simply cannot replace.

professor.name: Dr. Diaz
professor.text: A growing number of companies now pay employees cash bonuses or reduced insurance premiums for hitting fitness targets—logging gym visits, walking a set number of steps, or joining smoking-cessation programs. Supporters argue these incentives cut healthcare costs and build healthier habits, while critics say they penalize workers with chronic conditions and make private health an employer's business. Should companies use financial rewards to encourage healthier employee behavior?

students.name: Andrew
students.text: I think well-designed incentives can genuinely help. Many people know they should exercise but never start, and a modest financial nudge is often enough to establish a routine. If a company lowers everyone's premiums by keeping the workforce healthier, both the employer and its employees clearly benefit.

students.name: Kelly
students.text: These programs sound generous but quietly punish the people who need support most. An employee managing diabetes or a demanding schedule may never hit the targets, yet still watches healthier colleagues collect the rewards. Health outcomes depend on circumstances that a bonus cannot simply override.

professor.name: Dr. Gupta
professor.text: Several cities have recently begun offering completely fare-free public transit, letting anyone ride buses and trains without paying. Some economists argue this reduces traffic and car emissions while helping low-income commuters, while others contend it drains transit budgets and leads to overcrowded, poorly maintained systems. Should cities make public transportation free for all riders? Why or why not?

students.name: Andrew
students.text: I believe fare-free transit is a worthwhile investment. Ticketing systems are expensive to operate and enforce, so scrapping fares recovers part of the cost, and cheaper commuting draws drivers off congested roads. The environmental and economic gains to a city outweigh the lost fare revenue.

students.name: Paul
students.text: In my opinion, free transit is rarely as beneficial as it sounds. Fares fund maintenance and new routes, and without them systems gradually deteriorate until service becomes unreliable. Targeted subsidies for people who genuinely cannot afford tickets would help commuters far more than making every wealthy rider free.

professor.name: Dr. Achebe
professor.text: We've been discussing how brands reach young audiences this semester. A large share of advertising—from cereal mascots to app pop-ups—is aimed directly at children, who often cannot yet distinguish marketing from ordinary content. Some argue this exposure is a normal part of a commercial culture, while others believe children deserve legal protection from targeted ads. Should companies be allowed to advertise directly to young children? Why or why not?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think advertising to young children should be tightly restricted. Kids lack the cognitive maturity to recognize persuasive intent, so they absorb marketing messages as simple truth. Exploiting that vulnerability to sell sugary snacks or costly toys strikes me as fundamentally unfair, and clear regulation would protect families.

students.name: Kelly
students.text: A blanket ban would be both impractical and heavy-handed. Children encounter countless advertisements across television, games, and packaging, and parents—not regulators—are best positioned to guide what their kids watch and want. Teaching media literacy early does far more good than pretending advertising can be walled off entirely.
